lines.text: Alright, we're midnight now. {{ELAPSED}} in and the city's still burning. Let's start with the record that changed everything. Green Day, 'Basket Case,' off Dookie. 1994. The song that made a million kids realize they weren't alone in their heads.

Billie Joe Armstrong—the guy who wrote this—his old man died when he was ten. Fragmented his whole world. By the time he's writing 'Basket Case,' he's channeling that anxiety straight into three minutes of pure catharsis. That's what punk does. It takes your wreckage and makes it *sing*.

They recorded Dookie in three weeks flat at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley. Three weeks. You know what we've been doing for six hours? Watching Madison implode. They spent twenty-one days making the most important pop-punk album of all time. Twenty million copies worldwide. Twenty *million*.
'Do you have the time to listen to me whine?' First line. That's the whole deal right there. You're home. You're listening. I'm whining. We're all basket cases and we're doing it together.

NOFX. 'Linoleum.' 1996. Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin—three kids from Fairfax High in LA who decided the only way to survive was to stay poor, stay angry, and never, *ever* sign to a major label.

So Long and Thanks for All the Shoes came out in '97. Faster. Harder. The whole album is them basically saying 'we're not slowing down, we're not selling out, and if you think we're gonna play nice you haven't been paying attention.' I respect that. I *live* that.

Fat Mike's still running Fat Wreck Chords out of his garage or whatever, signing bands nobody else will touch. Propagandhi. Anti-Flag. The people who actually *mean* it. That's the difference between punk rock and the Purge, you know? Punk rock *chooses* chaos. The Purge just... happens.

They made the 'Leave It Alone' video and then just... didn't send it to MTV. Decided they didn't want to be part of the machine. I like that energy. I'm broadcasting on an illegal frequency on the worst night of the year. Same impulse, different century.

The Descendents. 'Hope,' off Milo Goes to College. 1982. This is the one where they recorded their own *farts* in the studio. I'm not making that up. They literally cheered each other on while—you know what, it's beautiful. It's punk rock in its purest form.

Milo actually went to UC San Diego. That's why the album's called what it is. These kids weren't singing about burning down the system or starting a revolution. They were singing about girls, about parents, about rejection. About *life*. That's harder than any political manifesto, honestly.

Bill Stevenson—drummer—he built The Blasting Room studio out in Fort Collins. That's where they recorded Everything Sucks in '96. Guy went from hardcore to businessman and somehow stayed punk the whole time. I don't know how he did that.

Their early stuff influenced skate punk, pop-punk, everything that came after. Cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts—that's the formula. You're home barricading your kitchen right now and these songs are four decades old and they still *work*. That's the thing about real songs. They survive everything.

tracks.title: And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea

lines.text: Propagandhi. 'And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea.' 1996. Less Talk, More Rock. These guys are from Manitoba—Portage la Prairie, then Winnipeg. Canadian prairie DIY hardcore scene. And they *still* sound meaner than most American bands.

Chris Hannah formed this band in 1986 with the explicit goal of not playing nice. Less Talk, More Rock came out and it was so pro-feminism, so 'gay positive' that the West Coast punk scene basically blacklisted them. Good. If punk doesn't make people uncomfortable, it's not doing its job.

Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords after they opened for NOFX in '92. Fat Mike has always had this thing where he signs bands that actually *mean it*. Not the biggest names. The ones with something to say. The ones who would rather starve than compromise.

Their whole deal is anarchism, Indigenous sovereignty, animal rights, taking down capitalism. Song after song after song. And they're not subtle about it. They're the spice wars on Arrakis and we're all just desert people trying to survive. Heavy, right? But honest.

Blink-182. 'Dammit.' 1997. Poway, California. Mark Hoppus, Tom DeLonge, Scott Raynor. Three kids who met at a Battle of the Bands and decided the world wasn't ridiculous enough—they'd make it more ridiculous.

Hoppus fell off a *lamppost* trying to impress DeLonge. Off a lamppost. Crutches for three weeks. That's the energy. That's Blink-182 in one image. They're not trying to save the world. They're trying to crack jokes and not die.

'Dude Ranch' came out in '97 and 'Dammit' hit like a truck. They weren't singing about social problems. They were singing about adolescent frustration and lewd acts. Simple arrangements. Straightforward. No pretense. Just three kids being idiots and making it *work*.

Enema of the State in '99 made them huge. The whole pop-punk second wave happened because of that record. And 'What's My Age Again?' had them running naked through LA. MTV couldn't look away. That's punk rock power, right there.

tracks.title: This Is the End (For Me It Never Began)

lines.text: Anti-Flag. 'This Is the End (For Me It Never Began).' 2002. Pittsburgh. Justin Sane and Pat Thetic started this in 1988 and spent every year after making sure everyone knows what they think about imperialism, about corporate power, about the Bush wars.

They met Fat Mike in 2000 at Warped Tour and signed to Fat Wreck Chords. Then in '03 they put out The Terror State—literally an album about Bush's war on terrorism. No subtlety. No apology. Just rage and facts and essays by Howard Zinn in the liner notes.

Chris No. 2 lost his sister to violence—she and her boyfriend were murdered. So Anti-Flag recorded an entire EP, raised money for the Center for Victims of Violent Crime. That's what solidarity looks like. That's what it means to actually *care*.

They signed to RCA in 2006 and got destroyed by the punk community for it. 'Sellouts.' Yeah, maybe. But they were also the only band with the reach to actually talk to people who weren't already punk rockers. Sometimes you gotta walk into the machine to break it from the inside. Sometimes you just gotta *try*.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide kill count at 00:00 marker: 104 (cumulative). Up 22 from this point last year. East Side holding 18% of total kills YoY.
BACKUP POWER ALERT: Widespread outages reported across East Side grid. Station operating on backup diesel. Equipment holding. Tim monitoring relay integrity.
Lake Mendota shoreline activity elevated. 3 drowning incidents reported to volunteer recovery crews between 00:00-00:15. Water temp holding at 31F. Survivability window minimal.
